method: Multi-agent analysis: 11 subsystem deep-readers, 4 bug hunters, 25 adversarial verifiers (default-to-refute), plus a dedicated two-reader + cross-checker pass over packages/desktop/src-tauri/src/lib.rs. Every finding below was traced in code at the cited file:line by at least two independent agents on 2026-07-02 (dev @ v26.7.203-dev). Verdicts: CONFIRMED = defective path personally traced by a verifier instructed to refute it, no mitigation found.
history_context: Apr-Jul 2026: 1,140 commits on dev; ~78% of non-release commits corrective; 52+ WebKit-memory fixes without convergence; watchdog threshold flip-flops within hours/days (#847/#850, #880/#886).

findings.title: Cloud sync is a self-sustaining infinite upload→merge→state-update→upload loop on both desktop and PWA, cross-amplified over the LAN relay
findings.evidence: Desktop performCloudUpload calls `await mergeDoc(result.uploadedBinary)` whenever the upload found a non-empty remote (sync.ts:1486-1488), and gdriveUploadSafe sets `mergedRemote: !!remoteBinary` unconditionally (packages/sync/src/cloud/gdrive.ts:174), so after the first successful upload every upload triggers a merge-back. The worker MERGE_DOC handler unconditionally ends in saveAndBroadcast (packages/desktop/src/lib/automerge.worker.ts:1121) which always emits STATE_UPDATE (worker:630) — even when the merge changed nothing. The cloud subscriber is `subscribe(() => scheduleCloudUpload(provider))` with no filter on event.mutation (sync.ts:1302-1304), so the merge-caused STATE_UPDATE schedules the next upload 2 s later (UPLOAD_DEBOUNCE_MS, sync.ts:311). The PWA has the identical cycle: mergeDoc(result.uploadedBinary) at packages/pwa/src/lib/sync.ts:693-695, unconditional saveAndBroadcast in MERGE_DOC (packages/pwa/src/lib/automerge.worker.ts:742→305-306), unfiltered subscriber (pwa sync.ts:634-636). Two reinforcing echoes make the loop unbreakable: (a) the GDrive Changes poll has no self-write filter — any change to the fileId, including our own upload ~5 s earlier, triggers a full download and mergeDoc (gdrive.ts:314-320); (b) with a relay client connected, every desktop MERGE_DOC also sends BROADCAST_REQUEST (worker:634-635), the PWA merges it (pwa sync.ts:113-128), its own STATE_UPDATE schedules a PWA cloud upload, which the desktop's Changes poll then re-downloads.
findings.failure_scenario: User connects Google Drive on desktop (and/or PWA) and then does nothing. After the first upload, the app enters a perpetual ~2-10 s cycle: full remote download + main-thread WASM double-load/merge/save (see separate finding) + full upload + worker A.load/A.merge/A.save + full IndexedDB rewrite + full state hydration + (with a phone paired) full-doc relay broadcast serialized as number[]. This runs 24/7 on an idle machine — continuous CPU and WASM/JS allocation churn in exactly the WebKit renderer whose memory growth the watchdog keeps fighting — and burns Google API quota until 403s appear, which then permanently kill change detection (separate finding).
findings.suggested_fix: Break the cycle at three points: (1) skip scheduleCloudUpload when the doc-change event's mutation is MERGE_DOC/REPLACE_DOC (the desktop subscriber already receives event.mutation; add the field to the PWA STATE_UPDATE); (2) record A.getHeads at last successful upload and skip uploads whose heads are unchanged; (3) after each upload record the Drive revision/md5 and have the Changes poll ignore changes that match the last self-write. Any one of these damps the loop; all three stop it.

(2) scheduleCloudUpload has no change-detection guard — no head comparison, no binary hash, nothing but a 2 s debounce (UPLOAD_DEBOUNCE_MS=2_000 at sync.ts:311; scheduler body sync.ts:1554-1617). findings.title: PWA→desktop LAN sync does not exist: the relay never delivers client-pushed docs to the desktop, and the PWA pushes its doc exactly once per connection
findings.evidence: handle_connection's Binary branch only stores and rebroadcasts to other WebSocket clients: `*state.current_doc.write().await = Some(bytes); state.broadcast_tx.send(bytes)` (lib.rs:9414-9419). There is no app.emit of doc bytes anywhere in the relay, and the invoke_handler list (lib.rs:11690-11750) exposes no command that reads current_doc back to the frontend (its only other uses are memory stats at 6293/6355 and broadcast_doc at 6410). On the PWA side, broadcastDoc() is invoked solely from ws.onopen via setTimeout (packages/pwa/src/lib/sync.ts:110); the doc-change subscriber only schedules cloud uploads (pwa sync.ts:634-636), so post-connect PWA mutations never reach the relay either. Additionally, after a desktop restart current_doc is None until the next desktop mutation while a client is connected (lib.rs:9399 serves only the cached doc; the worker broadcasts only inside saveAndBroadcast when relayClientCount>0, automerge.worker.ts:634), so a freshly connected phone receives nothing until the desktop next changes something.
findings.failure_scenario: User marks items read / saves / archives on the phone while on the same Wi-Fi as the desktop. Those changes never reach the desktop over the advertised local relay — convergence depends entirely on cloud sync being configured on BOTH devices. Users see one-directional 'sync', unexplained divergence between phone and desktop, and a freshly paired phone that stays empty after a desktop restart until the desktop happens to mutate its doc. This is a direct root cause of perceived provider-sync instability.
findings.suggested_fix: Have the Rust relay app.emit client-pushed doc bytes (or expose a get_relay_doc command polled/evented on push) so the desktop frontend can mergeDoc them; wire the PWA's doc-change subscriber to also call broadcastDoc; and trigger a desktop broadcast when sync-client-count rises so new clients receive the current doc even when the relay cache is empty.
findings.verification:
I traced every claimed link in the path and all of them hold in the code as written.

1. Relay never delivers client-pushed docs to the desktop. In `handle_connection` (packages/desktop/src-tauri/src/lib.rs:9412-9419), the `Message::Binary` branch does exactly two things: `*state.current_doc.write().await = Some(bytes.clone()); let _ = state.broadcast_tx.send(bytes);`. `broadcast_tx` subscribers are only other WebSocket connections (subscribe at lib.rs:9408); there is no `app.emit` of doc bytes anywhere in the relay (the only relay emits are `sync-client-count` at lib.rs:9395/9451). 2. No command reads the doc back. The full `invoke_handler` list (lib.rs:11690-11750) contains no command returning `current_doc` contents. Its only other uses are byte-length for memory stats (lib.rs:6293, 6355) and the write in `broadcast_doc` (lib.rs:6410-6411).

3. Desktop frontend has no receive path. packages/desktop/src/lib/sync.ts never opens a WebSocket to the relay (it only invokes `get_sync_client_count`, `get_sync_url`, etc.); the comment at sync.ts:278-280 confirms outbound-only design ("worker posts BROADCAST_REQUEST... calls invoke('broadcast_doc')"). findings.title: Post-scrape memory recovery destroys the main renderer BEFORE the scrape command returns — captured posts are discarded and throttle state is wiped, creating a scrape→recover→rescrape loop
findings.evidence: fb_scrape_feed ends with `drop(recycle_guard); maybe_recover_after_social_feed_scrape(...).await; Ok(())` (lib.rs:7812-7822; identical in ig_scrape_feed 8670-8681 and li_scrape_feed 9302-9313). maybe_recover_after_social_feed_scrape (lib.rs:5944-6051) calls recover_main_window (6012) when post_social_scrape_memory_recovery_reason fires (5892-5942; thresholds: WebKit footprint >= 2GiB + 768MiB growth, or pressure >= 35% of high, lib.rs:87-91 — routinely met per the 52-fix memory history, #880/#886 tuned this exact function). recover_main_window destroys the MAIN renderer webview (destroy_main_window_for_recovery, lib.rs:10367-10371). The renderer is the JS context that (a) is still awaiting the `invoke("fb_scrape_feed")` inside runBackgroundJob (packages/desktop/src/lib/fb-capture.ts:523-531), (b) holds all accumulated raw posts from the fb-feed-data events, and (c) only writes to the store AFTER the invoke resolves (fb-capture.ts:866 store.addItems). It also calls note_renderer_recovery_attempt first (6010), zeroing heartbeats and setting a recovery cooldown (lib.rs:2261-2267) that fails any queued scraper command at begin_job (2330-2348).
findings.failure_scenario: A ~10-minute FB/IG/LI scrape completes natively with dozens of posts; WebKit footprint is above the 2GiB threshold (the product's chronic state), so the recovery fires before the command returns. The renderer — holding all extracted posts, the pending invoke promise, and every module-level state (lastScrapeAt cooldowns, deferred-retry counters, coordinator latches) — is destroyed. Zero items are persisted, recordScrape() never runs, no provider-health attempt is recorded, and after the renderer reboots the poller (5-min startup delay) re-runs a full scrape with lastScrapeAt=0. If memory stays high, every scrape ends in a renderer teardown with zero items ever saved: user-visible window reloads, endless provider WebView churn, and a feed that silently never updates — the exact recurring instability + memory-churn signature.
findings.suggested_fix: Return Ok(()) (send the invoke response) before running maybe_recover_after_social_feed_scrape (spawn it detached with a delay), and/or have the JS side persist per-pass results incrementally so a renderer rebuild cannot discard a completed scrape. Also persist lastScrapeAt/provider cooldowns outside the renderer (e.g., alongside sync-health.json) so a recovery cannot reset scrape throttles.

findings.title: Memory preflight recycles ALL scraper windows with no active-session check and runs before both the JS job gate and the Rust session mutex — any trigger for provider B destroys provider A's in-flight scrape
findings.evidence: recycle_social_scraper_windows_except destroys every fb/ig/li scraper window with no check of the scraper session or active_job (lib.rs:6053-6067), even though a guarded variant exists and is used only by the watchdog (recycle_social_scraper_windows_unless_active, lib.rs:647-664). The JS preflight (memory-monitor.ts:496 invoking prepare_social_scrape_memory, which passes preserve_label=None, lib.rs:6348-6371) runs BEFORE the runBackgroundJob gate in every capture (fb-capture.ts:403 vs 523; instagram-capture.ts:213 vs 284; li-capture.ts:183 vs 248), and ensure_social_scrape_memory runs BEFORE acquire_background_scraper_session in every Rust command (fb_scrape_feed lib.rs:7515-7523; ig 8396-8404; li 9113-9121; fb_check_auth 7226-7234 with preserve Some("fb-scraper") still killing ig/li).
findings.failure_scenario: FB is 5 minutes into a hidden-WebView scrape (holding the Rust mutex and JS activeJob). An IG deferred-retry timer fires (capture.ts:208-212), or the user clicks 'Sync now'/'Check connection' for another provider, or a dev-sync trigger arrives: the new trigger's preflight immediately window.destroy()s fb-scraper mid-scroll. FB's run then fails at extract/invoke or produces extract_empty; the killer itself then defers on the activeJob gate and schedules another retry, which can kill FB's next attempt too — a self-sustaining kill loop. Repeated kills feed the failure heuristics, waste 20-min cooldown windows, and multiply WebKit process churn.
findings.suggested_fix: Route the preflight recycle through recycle_social_scraper_windows_unless_active (or check the scraper_session mutex/active_job before destroying), pass each command's own window label as preserve_label, and move the JS preflight to AFTER the runBackgroundJob gate so it cannot fire while another scrape is active.

The guarded variant recycle_social_scraper_windows_unless_active (lib.rs:647-664) is used only by watchdog paths (lib.rs:11208, 11591, 11615), never by the preflight. findings.title: Post-login auto-sync destroys the IG/LinkedIn LOGIN window mid-login: login windows reuse the scraper label and loggedIn=true is emitted on the FIRST navigation away from /login
findings.evidence: ig_show_login builds the visible login window with label "ig-scraper" (lib.rs:8249-8252) and li_show_login with "li-scraper" (lib.rs:8953-8956) — both members of SOCIAL_SCRAPER_WINDOW_LABELS (lib.rs:68). The on_navigation handler emits `ig-auth-result {loggedIn:true}` on the first navigation whose path is not /accounts/login (lib.rs:8276-8282; LI: 8980-8987) — before 2FA/checkpoint/'save login' prompts complete. usePostLoginAutoSync starts runSync() on every loggedIn=true event (usePostLoginAutoSync.ts:97-123), runSync calls captureIgFeed directly (InstagramSettingsSection.tsx:132-140,157-167), and fetchIgFeed's memory preflight (instagram-capture.ts:213 → prepare_social_scrape_memory, preserve None) destroys ALL scraper windows — including the visible ig-scraper login window the user is still using. handleAuthResult also wholesale-writes {isAuthenticated:true, lastCheckedAt} (InstagramSettingsSection.tsx:144-147), so a premature nav (e.g. LinkedIn /checkpoint/challenge) marks a NOT-logged-in session authenticated.
findings.failure_scenario: User connects LinkedIn; after credentials, LinkedIn redirects to /checkpoint/challenge for 2FA. The nav handler fires loggedIn=true; the hook starts a full LI sync within milliseconds; the preflight destroys the li-scraper window while the user is typing the 2FA code. Login is aborted, yet liAuth.isAuthenticated=true is persisted. Every subsequent scheduled scrape runs against a logged-out session, which LI/IG never classify as an auth failure (see separate finding), producing eternal empty scrapes and the recurring 'provider login prompts closed' regressions (#706/#832 fixed the deliberate close path but not this recycle path).
findings.suggested_fix: Give IG/LI login windows dedicated labels (like fb-login) excluded from SOCIAL_SCRAPER_WINDOW_LABELS; verify auth with page evidence (cookie check) before emitting loggedIn=true instead of first-navigation heuristics; and have the preflight skip recycling any visible login window.

findings.title: Cloud sync is a self-sustaining infinite download/merge/upload loop with no damping
findings.evidence: The doc-change subscriber is `subscribe(() => { scheduleCloudUpload(provider); })` with no filter on the event's mutation source (packages/desktop/src/lib/sync.ts:1302-1304). performCloudUpload runs gdriveUploadSafe and then `if (result.mergedRemote) { ... await mergeDoc(result.uploadedBinary); }` (sync.ts:1486-1489). gdriveUploadSafe sets `mergedRemote: !!remoteBinary` (packages/sync/src/cloud/gdrive.ts:174) — true whenever the remote file is non-empty, i.e. after every upload forever, regardless of whether content changed. Worker MERGE_DOC unconditionally ends in `await saveAndBroadcast(trace)` (packages/desktop/src/lib/automerge.worker.ts:1121) which always emits STATE_UPDATE (worker:630), re-triggering the subscriber. Separately, the GDrive Changes poll matches only `c.fileId === fileId` with no self-write filtering (gdrive.ts:314-320), so each own upload is re-downloaded via gdriveDownloadLatest ~5 s later (gdrive.ts:318-321) and fed to onRemoteChange → mergeDoc (sync.ts:1201-1204), which again emits STATE_UPDATE → scheduleCloudUpload. The PWA has the identical cycle (packages/pwa/src/lib/sync.ts:634-636 subscriber, 693-695 mergeDoc after upload). mergeBinaries runs A.load×2 + A.merge + A.save on the MAIN renderer thread (packages/sync/src/cloud/merge.ts:18-33, called from sync.ts:1485 / pwa sync.ts:692).
findings.failure_scenario: Any device with GDrive connected, completely idle: upload completes → mergeDoc(uploadedBinary) → STATE_UPDATE → scheduleCloudUpload → 2 s debounce (sync.ts:311) → full remote download + main-thread WASM merge (2 full docs + merged copy in renderer WASM memory) + full upload → mergeDoc → ... forever, every ~5-10 s. The Changes poll doubles the cycle. With a PWA relay-connected, each MERGE_DOC also fires a full-doc relay broadcast (worker:634-636), and the phone's mergeDoc → STATE_UPDATE → its own cloud upload — cross-device amplification. Continuous multi-MB allocations in the WebKit renderer while 'idle' is a direct, quantifiable root cause of the recurring renderer memory growth, plus Google API quota burn (403s then kill the poll loop one-way).
findings.suggested_fix: Filter the subscriber on event.mutation (skip MERGE_DOC/REPLACE_DOC/UPDATE_LAST_SYNC — the event already carries mutation, packages/desktop/src/lib/automerge.ts:301-309); record A.getHeads at last upload and skip uploads when heads are unchanged; record own Drive revision/md5 after upload so the Changes poll ignores self-writes; move mergeBinaries into the Automerge worker.

I traced every link of the claimed loop and found no mitigation that breaks it.

Desktop cycle (all links verified):
1. Unfiltered subscriber: packages/desktop/src/lib/sync.ts:1302-1304 is exactly `subscribe(() => { scheduleCloudUpload(provider); })`. The subscriber signature receives `(state, event)` where event carries `source` and `mutation` (packages/desktop/src/lib/automerge.ts:235, 266-272, 301-309) — the mutation type "MERGE_DOC" is available and could be used to break the cycle, but the sync.ts callback ignores it.
2. Upload always re-merges: performCloudUpload (sync.ts:1482-1489) calls gdriveUploadSafe, and `if (result.mergedRemote) { await mergeDoc(result.uploadedBinary); }`. gdriveUploadSafe sets `mergedRemote: !!remoteBinary` (packages/sync/src/cloud/gdrive.ts:174), which is true whenever the Drive file is non-empty — i.e., on every upload after the first, forever, regardless of whether anything changed. There is no heads/hash/byte comparison anywhere.
3. The main-thread handler (automerge.ts:301-309) publishes to all subscribers → back to step 1. After the 2s debounce (UPLOAD_DEBOUNCE_MS, sync.ts:311) the next upload fires → step 2. findings.title: Every synced mutation ships the full doc binary as a number[] through two JS heaps plus JSON Tauri IPC (~16-20x doc size transient)
findings.evidence: saveAndBroadcast and persistAndBroadcastWithoutHydration both do `send({ type: "BROADCAST_REQUEST", data: Array.from(binary) })` whenever relayClientCount > 0 (packages/desktop/src/lib/automerge.worker.ts:634-636 and 708-710) — this runs on EVERY doc mutation including single mark-as-read (applyItemPatchChange → persistAndBroadcastWithoutHydration, worker:813). The main thread structured-clones the number[] and calls `invoke("broadcast_doc", { docBytes: msg.data })` (packages/desktop/src/lib/automerge.ts:367-372, errors swallowed). Rust receives `doc_bytes: Vec<u8>` as a plain command arg (packages/desktop/src-tauri/src/lib.rs:6405-6408), i.e. the payload crosses Tauri IPC as a JSON array of numbers, and is then retained forever in `state.current_doc` as Arc<Vec<u8>> (lib.rs:6409-6410, 2054).
findings.failure_scenario: With a phone paired and a 20 MB doc: each mutation allocates ~160 MB (number[] in worker, ~8 B/element) + ~160 MB (structured-cloned number[] on the renderer main thread — same WebContent process on WKWebView) + a ~80-160 MB JSON string for invoke serialization. During a content-fetch backlog (one mutation every 2.5-7.5 s, content-fetcher.ts:41-42) or the cloud upload loop, this repeats continuously — hundreds of MB of transient allocations per cycle in exactly the WebKit renderer whose RSS growth the watchdog keeps killing.
findings.suggested_fix: Post the Uint8Array itself from the worker (structured clone of a typed array is byte-exact 1x, or transfer the buffer of a copy), and use a Tauri 2 raw-payload invoke (ArrayBuffer body / tauri::ipc::Request with InvokeBody::Raw) or a Channel instead of a JSON number array; also clear current_doc when the last client disconnects.

I personally traced every link in the claimed chain and found no mitigation anywhere.

1. Full doc binary shipped per mutation: `saveAndBroadcast` sends `{ type: "BROADCAST_REQUEST", data: Array.from(binary) }` when `relayClientCount > 0` (packages/desktop/src/lib/automerge.worker.ts:634-636), and `persistAndBroadcastWithoutHydration` does the same (automerge.worker.ts:708-710). So incremental persistence does not shrink the broadcast payload.

findings.title: Automerge history grows without bound; the only history-dropping rebuild is gated on an oversized-text edge case and the PWA has none
findings.evidence: compactLoadedFeedText only rebuilds a fresh doc when `summary.changed > 0 && previousBinaryBytes >= FRESH_DOC_REBUILD_MIN_CHANGED_BINARY_BYTES` (4 MB) (packages/desktop/src/lib/automerge.worker.ts:280-282, constant at 102) — i.e. only when oversized text was actually trimmed during INIT/REPLACE_DOC/MERGE_DOC. On a healthy doc summary.changed is 0 and the rebuild never fires; there is no scheduled or size-triggered compaction anywhere. persistDoc's snapshot path is plain A.save (packages/desktop/src/lib/automerge-persistence.ts:62), which retains the full operation history. pruneArchivedItems only does `delete doc.feedItems[id]` inside A.change (packages/shared/src/schema.ts:1458-1465), leaving tombstones and all prior ops in history. findings.title: Every synced mutation ships the whole Automerge doc as a boxed number[] through two JS heaps plus JSON IPC
findings.evidence: saveAndBroadcast and persistAndBroadcastWithoutHydration both do `if (relayClientCount > 0) { send({ type: "BROADCAST_REQUEST", data: Array.from(binary) }); }` (automerge.worker.ts:634-636, 708-710) — the full doc binary as a plain JS number array. The main thread then runs `invoke("broadcast_doc", { docBytes: msg.data })` (automerge.ts:367-372), and Rust receives `doc_bytes: Vec<u8>` (lib.rs:6403-6413), i.e. the payload crosses Tauri IPC as a JSON array of numbers. Every mutation path funnels here: applyChange (worker:735), applyItemPatchChange (worker:813), applyPreferenceChange (worker:753), each 50ms-batched read-mark flush, and each background article update (content-fetcher.ts:543 -> UPDATE_FEED_ITEM -> applyChange at worker:1256-1266).
findings.failure_scenario: With a phone paired over the relay, a 30MB doc mutation transiently allocates ~240MB (number[] in the worker heap), ~240MB again (structured clone in the main WebKit renderer), plus a multi-hundred-MB JSON string for invoke serialization — per mutation, including one per 2.5-7.5s during content-fetch backlogs and per 120ms read-mark flush while scrolling. Repeated multi-hundred-MB transient allocations in the renderer are a direct quantifiable driver of the WebKit RSS growth the watchdog then 'fixes' by recycling the renderer.
findings.suggested_fix: Send the binary as a transferable Uint8Array/ArrayBuffer from the worker and use a Tauri command that accepts raw bytes (tauri::ipc::Request / InvokeBody::Raw), and debounce relay broadcasts (1-2s) instead of broadcasting per mutation; longer term switch to Automerge sync-protocol increments.

findings.title: PWA connect() orphans live sockets: the old socket's onclose clobbers the new socket reference and leaves an unreachable connection that keeps merging docs
findings.evidence: connect() does `if (ws) ws.close()` then immediately `ws = new WebSocket(url)` (sync.ts:90-99). WebSocket close events are dispatched asynchronously, so the OLD socket's onclose always fires after `ws` already points at the NEW socket, and it unconditionally executes `isRelayConnectedState = false; ws = null` (sync.ts:130-133) — nulling the reference to the new, still-connecting socket — then schedules another connect() in 5 s (sync.ts:138-147). The orphaned new socket keeps its onopen/onmessage handlers (onopen even sets isRelayConnectedState=true, suppressing the 5 s reconnect), but broadcastDoc (sync.ts:66) and disconnect() (sync.ts:169-172) only see the module-level `ws`, which is null.
findings.failure_scenario: User re-pairs or reconnects from SyncConnectDialog (SyncConnectDialog.tsx:245/317 call connect()) while a connection exists. Result: an open socket that (a) can never be closed by disconnect(), (b) keeps invoking mergeDoc for every relay frame in parallel with any later tracked socket — duplicate full-doc merges per broadcast and inflated desktop client counts — and (c) never sends the initial doc push (broadcastDoc sees ws===null). Repeated re-pairing accumulates orphans; 'Disconnect' in settings silently fails to stop syncing.
findings.suggested_fix: In onclose/onerror handlers, capture the socket they belong to and only null the module state if `ws === thisSocket` (identity check) before mutating state or scheduling reconnect; same guard in onopen.
findings.verification: Personally traced the full defective path. connect() (packages/pwa/src/lib/sync.ts:89-99) closes the old socket and synchronously reassigns `ws` to a new WebSocket; the old socket's close event is dispatched asynchronously, so its onclose (sync.ts:130-148) always runs after reassignment and, with no socket-identity guard, executes `isRelayConnectedState = false; ws = null` (sync.ts:132-133) — destroying the module reference to the new, live socket — then schedules a 5s reconnect. findings.title: A single 403 (rate limit) or transient startup error permanently kills GDrive change detection for the session, with uploads continuing — silent one-way divergence
findings.evidence: gdriveStartPollLoop throws on 401/403 from the changes endpoint (packages/sync/src/cloud/gdrive.ts:305-307 and 327-328) and on any startup failure (startPageToken fetch at gdrive.ts:275-278, which also takes no abort signal). runGDrivePollLoop rethrows anything that is not 401/403 (`if (status !== 401 && status !== 403) throw error;` desktop sync.ts:1255 — note a plain network error has no status), and for 403 calls refreshCloudTokenAfterAuthFailure which itself throws for any status ≠ 401 ('auth failure is not refreshable', sync.ts:604-609) and also throws when inside the 5-minute refresh cooldown (sync.ts:617-622). Every such path lands in `runGDrivePollLoop().catch(...)` (sync.ts:1265-1280) which only marks an error — the loop is never restarted. findings.failure_scenario: Google returns one 403 for rate limiting — made likely by the infinite upload loop — or the machine wakes offline and the startPageToken fetch rejects. Change detection dies for the rest of the session while uploads keep succeeding: the device pushes its state but silently stops receiving other devices' changes until app restart or manual reconnect. Users experience unexplained divergence between devices.
findings.suggested_fix: Restart the poll loop with capped exponential backoff on non-auth and cooldown-suppressed errors instead of letting the error escape runGDrivePollLoop; treat 403 rate-limit responses (reason=rateLimitExceeded/userRateLimitExceeded) as retryable backoff rather than auth failures.

findings.title: initDoc failure handling wipes the entire local document store on ANY init error, including transient IndexedDB failures
findings.evidence: PWA initDoc: `initPromise = sendInit().catch(async () => { await clearLocalDoc(); return sendInit(); })` (pwa automerge.ts:254-257); desktop initDoc has the identical pattern (desktop automerge.ts:547-561). The worker INIT handler already handles the corrupt-document case itself (A.load wrapped in try/catch that clears storage, pwa worker:334-341, desktop worker:1004-1022), so the errors that actually reach this main-thread catch are storage-layer failures — e.g. a transient IndexedDB open/read error. Those trigger CLEAR_LOCAL → storage.clear(), deleting the persisted Automerge binary. Aggravating: IndexedDBStorage.save resolves on request.onsuccess rather than transaction.oncomplete (packages/sync/src/storage/indexeddb.ts:63-64), so a commit-time abort (Safari quota pressure) is reported as success, and the cached dbPromise (indexeddb.ts:13-31) has no onclose recovery, so a closed connection makes every subsequent operation fail until reload.
findings.failure_scenario: iOS Safari under storage pressure momentarily fails the IndexedDB open during PWA startup. sendInit rejects, initDoc silently deletes the entire local library — all unsynced changes, and with cloud sync off, all data — then recreates an empty doc. Combined with the empty-library adoption finding, the emptied device then wholesale-adopts whatever remote doc arrives next.
findings.suggested_fix: Distinguish 'document corrupt' (worker already clears) from 'storage transiently unavailable': retry sendInit with backoff on storage errors instead of clearLocalDoc; snapshot the binary before any wipe; resolve IndexedDB saves on transaction.oncomplete and invalidate dbPromise on db.onclose.

findings.title: Full-document CRDT merge (A.load ×2 + A.merge + A.save) runs on the MAIN renderer thread for every cloud upload, defeating the Web Worker migration
findings.evidence: mergeBinaries loads both full binaries into Automerge WASM, merges, runs the destructive-merge guard, and saves (merge.ts:18-33). It is called inside gdriveUploadSafe (gdrive.ts:156) and dropboxUploadSafe (dropbox.ts:65), which desktop performCloudUpload (desktop sync.ts:1485) and PWA performCloudUpload (pwa sync.ts:692) invoke directly on the main thread — not in the Automerge worker where all other WASM work was deliberately moved (per the worker-migration comments in both automerge.ts files).
findings.failure_scenario: Every debounced upload materializes two full document copies plus a merged copy in main-thread WASM linear memory (which never shrinks once grown). Because the upload feedback loop runs continuously, this executes every few seconds forever, holding and regrowing main-renderer WASM memory — a direct, code-level contributor to the recurring WebKit renderer memory growth that the renderer-recycling watchdog fights, and main-thread jank on large libraries.
findings.suggested_fix: Move the download→merge→upload cycle (or at minimum mergeBinaries) into the Automerge worker, which already has the local doc loaded — eliminating one of the three copies and all main-thread WASM work; alternatively skip the merge+upload entirely when the downloaded remote binary equals the last-uploaded binary.

findings.evidence: Rust x_api_request returns Err("X API error {status}: {body}") on any non-2xx (lib.rs:4512-4516). fetchXTimeline's catch sets errorStage="transport" (x-capture.ts:229-232). The only path that clears cookies and flips isAuthenticated=false is stage "auth" (x-capture.ts:509-518), which fires solely for JSON-body error codes 32/89/135/326 on a 200 response (x-capture.ts:253-259). findings.failure_scenario: X revokes/expires the user's auth_token (the common expiry mode is HTTP 401). xAuth.isAuthenticated remains true, localStorage cookies persist, and every 30-min poll plus every manual sync refires the dead request indefinitely. The feed silently stops updating; provider-health records an endless run of 'error/transport' attempts that never pause the provider and never transition the account to a reconnect state (only the broad lastCaptureError regex in provider-auth-errors.ts happens to match '401' in settings UI).
findings.suggested_fix: Parse the HTTP status out of the Rust error (or return a structured status) and map 401/403 to stage 'auth' so cookies are cleared and isAuthenticated flips, stopping the dead-cookie retry loop.

findings.title: 'Check connection' misclassifies a busy scraper or post-recovery cooldown as 'logged out', wipes the provider auth state wholesale, and each successful check fires an unsolicited full feed sync
findings.evidence: checkFbAuth resolves false on a 15s timeout and on any invoke rejection, and registers its listener via .then without awaiting registration before invoking (fb-auth.ts:63-85; identical instagram-auth.ts:52-74, li-auth.ts:53-75). Rust fb_check_auth blocks on the global scraper mutex, which an active scrape holds for minutes (lib.rs:7234), and begin_job rejects with transient runtime errors (heartbeats/recovery-cooldown/memory-cooldown, lib.rs:2330-2384) — e.g. immediately after a post-scrape renderer recovery (lib.rs:6010). handleCheckAuth then persists `{ isAuthenticated: loggedIn, lastCheckedAt }` wholesale, discarding lastCapturedAt/pause/error fields (FacebookSettingsSection.tsx:349-352; InstagramSettingsSection.tsx:185-188). Separately, every fb/ig/li-auth-result loggedIn=true event triggers runSync via usePostLoginAutoSync (usePostLoginAutoSync.ts:97-123), so a plain check click also launches a full scrape; fb_check_auth's preflight additionally recycles the OTHER providers' scraper windows (lib.rs:7226-7233, preserve Some("fb-scraper")).
findings.failure_scenario: User clicks 'Check connection' while any scrape is running: ig/li windows are destroyed (killing an in-flight scrape), the check times out at 15s, a healthy logged-in account is stored as isAuthenticated:false with its pause/history wiped, and scheduled syncs for it silently stop (refreshSocialProvider gates on isAuthenticated, capture.ts:250-264). Minutes later the queued Rust check finally runs, emits loggedIn=true, flips the state back AND starts an unsolicited full scrape — a confusing disconnect/reconnect/scrape sequence from one click.
findings.suggested_fix: Await listen() registration before invoking; have Rust emit a distinct 'busy/deferred' result instead of letting the JS timeout mean 'logged out'; merge check results into existing auth state instead of replacing it; and gate the post-login hook's runSync to actual login-window flows rather than every auth-result event.

findings.title: Idle-unload resets persistence state on every queue drain, so the incremental-append binary is never compacted — persisted doc grows as an unbounded chain of incremental chunks
findings.evidence: scheduleDocIdleUnload runs in the .finally of every request (worker:1599-1602) and, whenever the queue is empty, does `persistenceState = createPersistenceState(currentBinary)` (worker:226-234); ensureCurrentDocLoaded does the same on reload (worker:242). createPersistenceState sets baseSnapshotBytes = full concatenated binary length and incrementalBytesSinceSnapshot = 0 (automerge-persistence.ts:18-24). persistDoc only re-encodes with A.save when `incrementalBytesSinceSnapshot + incremental >= baseSnapshotBytes` (automerge-persistence.ts:56-59). Under isolated mutations (content-fetcher fires one every 2.5-7.5 s; read marks; the cloud loop), the counter is reset to 0 after every single mutation while previously-appended chunks are laundered into the base, so compaction requires ONE incremental larger than the whole binary — effectively never.
findings.failure_scenario: Each isolated mutation appends an incremental chunk (~hundreds of bytes of header + ops) to currentBinary via concatUint8Arrays (automerge-persistence.ts:70) and the whole growing blob is rewritten to IndexedDB. A content-fetch backlog adds ~500-1,400 chunks/hour; the binary is never re-encoded into columnar form (which A.save would compress dramatically), so on-disk size, A.load time on every worker reload, relay broadcast size, and cloud upload size all grow strictly faster than actual history growth. The intended 2x-snapshot bound on concatenation is dead code in practice.
findings.suggested_fix: Preserve baseSnapshotBytes/incrementalBytesSinceSnapshot across idle unload/reload (store them alongside currentBinary instead of calling createPersistenceState), or compact with a full A.save before releasing the idle doc.

findings.title: runBackgroundJob timeout clears activeJob while the underlying invoke keeps running; combined with the untimed Rust mutex queue this produces 'ghost scrapes' whose events have no listeners
findings.evidence: The job races task.run against a timer (background-runtime-coordinator.ts:316-328); on timeout the finally block unconditionally sets activeJob=null and wakes waiters (335-341) while the native invoke (a scraper WebView run of up to ~10 minutes) continues detached. Callers remove their event listeners in their own finally (fb-capture.ts:544-547), so subsequent fb-feed-data events are dropped. On the Rust side, queued commands wait on the session mutex with no deadline (lib.rs:4123-4127), so a scrape whose JS caller gave up still runs its full scroll sequence later.
findings.failure_scenario: A slow FB scrape hits the 600s JS timeout (or the 180s rss-poll timeout for the wrapper): the coordinator believes nothing is running, the next queued capture starts and its preflight recycles the still-running scrape's window (finding #2), and any commands already queued behind the Rust mutex (e.g. an IG scrape triggered meanwhile) later execute complete scrapes whose results are emitted to nobody — burning provider rate-limit budget and WebKit memory with zero items persisted, and recording confusing 'invoke' errors in provider-health.
findings.suggested_fix: On timeout, keep activeJob held until task.run actually settles (the side-effect-scheduler already implements this settled-promise pattern), and add a deadline to acquire_background_scraper_session so stale queued scraper commands abort instead of running unobserved.

findings.evidence: Write lock acquired at 11251, held until the block returns at 11588. findings.evidence: should_recover (11303-11309) uses only recovery_allowed + age/lag + per-attempt cooldown; recover_main_window fires unconditionally at 11598-11601, unlike the memory monitor's active_job.is_none() gate (11055) and the scraper-recycle deferral (647-664, 11590-11596). Extraction results are fire-and-forget events consumed by TS listeners in the main renderer (fb-capture.ts:455 listens to 'fb-feed-data'; li-extract.js:414 emits 'li-feed-data'); fb_scrape_feed returns Ok after emitting passes (7803-7822). CORRECTION to the secondary race: fb_scrape_feed does NOT drop its scraper session before maybe_recover_after_social_feed_scrape — only drop(wv); drop(recycle_guard) at 7812-7813, with `_scraper_session` (7523) held to end of function; the drop(scraper_session)-before-recover gap exists only in ig_scrape_feed (8670-8679) and li_scrape_feed (9302-9310). maybe_recover itself calls recover_main_window with no job gate (5995-6012).
findings.failure_scenario: A visible renderer stale for 75s mid-scrape is destroyed while the scraper keeps emitting into a void; the command returns Ok and the provider sync 'completes' with zero posts. The back-to-back-provider race (next provider begins during post-scrape recovery) is real for IG and LI scrapes, not FB.
findings.verification: A visible renderer stale for 75s mid-scrape is destroyed while the scraper keeps emitting into a void; the command returns Ok and the provider sync 'completes' with zero posts. The back-to-back-provider race (next provider begins during post-scrape recovery) is real for IG and LI scrapes, not FB.

findings.title: VERIFIED (A3): WebKit process attribution cannot distinguish the main renderer from scraper/login WebContent; the restart-escalating verification's test fabricates a role production never emits
findings.evidence: freed_webkit_process_role (5231-5247) returns only 'freed-webcontent' (any WebContent with an open file under app storage roots — Caches/App Support dirs enumerated at 5165-5188 — started after app start) or 'freed-webcontent-age-matched'; no per-window discrimination exists. main_renderer_memory_recovery_reason keys on exactly these roles (2981-2988, min-age gate 2990-2996) and main_renderer_recovery_verification_should_restart on the same via main_renderer_webkit_role_can_recover (3093-3132). The unit test at 3609 sets webkit_largest_role: Some("fb-scraper".to_string()) — a string no production path produces. fb-login is a long-lived separate-label window (built at 7142-7185) absent from SOCIAL_SCRAPER_WINDOW_LABELS (68: fb-scraper/ig-scraper/li-scraper only), so no recycle path ever touches it.
findings.failure_scenario: When scraper/login WebContent is the largest WebKit process, the memory monitor destroys the main window (reclaiming nothing) and verification 3s later (same PID, <512MiB reclaimed, still >= high — 3120-3132) triggers a full app restart via request_restart_after_recovery_verification_failure (10270-10285, 10175-10213), never touching the actual hog.
findings.verification: When scraper/login WebContent is the largest WebKit process, the memory monitor destroys the main window (reclaiming nothing) and verification 3s later (same PID, <512MiB reclaimed, still >= high — 3120-3132) triggers a full app restart via request_restart_after_recovery_verification_failure (10270-10285, 10175-10213), never touching the actual hog.

findings.title: VERIFIED (A4): cpu_usage is always 0.0 — hot-CPU recovery is dead code and every cpu-idle gate is vacuous
findings.evidence: collect_runtime_memory_stats_with_options builds System::new() per call (5366) and both refreshes use ProcessRefreshKind::nothing().with_memory() (5368-5372, 5385-5389) — no CPU refresh, and sysinfo requires two spaced CPU refreshes for non-zero readings; process.cpu_usage() read at 5313. Gates confirmed: cpu >= 20.0 required by main_renderer_hot_webkit_activity_should_recover (3080-3083, constant at 122) — can never fire; cpu <= 10.0 idle gates at 3049-3052, 3064-3067, and webkit_resident_tail_is_probably_reclaimable at 5632-5639 — always pass; scrape_effective_resident_bytes then substitutes footprint for resident (5642-5649) feeding scrape_memory_may_proceed (5651-5653).
findings.failure_scenario: The 'webkit_hot_active_pressure' trigger is unreachable; busy WebContent is systematically classified as idle/reclaimable, letting scrapes start under genuine pressure and idle-tail recoveries fire against actively-working renderers.
findings.verification: The 'webkit_hot_active_pressure' trigger is unreachable; busy WebContent is systematically classified as idle/reclaimable, letting scrapes start under genuine pressure and idle-tail recoveries fire against actively-working renderers.

findings.title: VERIFIED (A5): fb_check_group_membership leaks its event listener on every failure path including the 10s timeout; fb_scrape_groups leaks per-pass listeners on eval/payload errors
findings.evidence: fb_check_group_membership: listener registered at 8058; wv.eval(...).map_err(...)? at 8151-8152 returns before unlisten; all three match arms at 8154-8158 (Ok(Ok(result)) => result?, Ok(Err(_)) => Err(...)?, Err(_) => Err("...timed out after 10 seconds")?) propagate before the sole app.unlisten at 8160. fb_scrape_groups: per-pass listener at 7905; eval ? at 7921-7922 leaks; Ok(Ok(result)) => result? at 7925 leaks on payload error; only channel-closed (7927) and timeout (7931) arms unlisten before erroring, success path unlistens at 7936.
findings.failure_scenario: Each leaked closure (Arc<Mutex<Option<Sender>>> + captured handles) stays in the global event registry for process lifetime; routine timeouts on slow Facebook pages accumulate listeners without bound — steady Rust-heap growth plus growing dispatch fanout on those events.
findings.verification: Each leaked closure (Arc<Mutex<Option<Sender>>> + captured handles) stays in the global event registry for process lifetime; routine timeouts on slow Facebook pages accumulate listeners without bound — steady Rust-heap growth plus growing dispatch fanout on those events.

findings.title: VERIFIED (A6): IG and LI login windows reuse scraper labels and are destroyed mid-login by other providers' preflights, critical-pressure cleanup, and watchdog recycles
findings.evidence: ig_show_login builds its visible login window with label "ig-scraper" (8249-8253, after recycling it at 8243); li_show_login uses "li-scraper" (8953-8956). Both are in SOCIAL_SCRAPER_WINDOW_LABELS (68). recycle_social_scraper_windows_except destroys all non-preserved labels unconditionally (6053-6069) and runs in every provider's memory preflight (6093-6094); the watchdog recycles all scraper labels when the main renderer goes stale via recycle_social_scraper_windows_unless_active (11590-11596) whose only protection is an active fb_/ig_/li_ background job (637-664) — login flows acquire no scraper session (ig_show_login/li_show_login absent from the acquire_background_scraper_session call list), so nothing protects them. Facebook alone uses the separate "fb-login" label (7144).
findings.failure_scenario: A user mid-login on Instagram/LinkedIn has the window destroyed whenever another provider's scrape preflight or a renderer hiccup fires — randomly failing connect flows feeding provider-sync-instability reports.
findings.verification: A user mid-login on Instagram/LinkedIn has the window destroyed whenever another provider's scrape preflight or a renderer hiccup fires — randomly failing connect flows feeding provider-sync-instability reports.

findings.title: VERIFIED (A7): WebContent processes failing role attribution are excluded from ALL memory totals, under-counting real WebKit usage in scrape budgets
findings.evidence: freed_webkit_memory_stats skips any WebKit.WebContent whose role is None via `else { continue; }` (5294-5298) — i.e. no lsof match under app storage roots AND age not within 10s of app start (5231-5247, grace constant at 92). app_storage_roots (5165-5188) enumerates only app_cache_dir, app_data_dir, and ~/Library/{Caches,Application Support}/{wtf.freed.desktop,freed-desktop} — no ~/Library/WebKit path where custom data_store_identifier stores (69-77) keep website data. Excluded processes contribute nothing to webkit_total_resident/footprint and hence nothing to app_memory_pressure_bytes (5418-5424).
findings.failure_scenario: scrape_memory_may_proceed (5651-5653), per-pass continue checks, and post-scrape recovery reasons evaluate totals that can omit the very WebContent doing the scraping; combined with A3, attribution is wrong in both directions.
findings.verification: scrape_memory_may_proceed (5651-5653), per-pass continue checks, and post-scrape recovery reasons evaluate totals that can omit the very WebContent doing the scraping; combined with A3, attribution is wrong in both directions.

findings.title: VERIFIED (B1): relay never detects dead clients — no server-side ping, count decremented only on task exit
findings.evidence: handle_connection's loop (9410-9443) exits only on Close/None/Err from ws_receiver or a failed ws_sender.send; the server only answers client Pings with Pongs (9424-9426) and never initiates Ping; client_count decrement happens after the loop (9445-9452). A half-open connection parks the task in ws_receiver.next() forever; a stuck ws_sender.send at 9436 blocks holding a full doc copy. Downstream gating confirmed: sync.ts polls get_sync_client_count every 2s and forwards via setRelayClientCount; the worker emits BROADCAST_REQUEST with Array.from(binary) whenever relayClientCount>0 (automerge.worker.ts:634-635, 708-709), landing in broadcast_doc's Vec<u8> JSON-array IPC (6405-6413).
findings.failure_scenario: One dead phone connection permanently converts every doc save into O(doc-size) serialization churn in the WebKit renderer with zero live clients — a standing contributor to the recurring renderer memory growth, plus leaked connection tasks pinning doc copies.
findings.verification: One dead phone connection permanently converts every doc save into O(doc-size) serialization churn in the WebKit renderer with zero live clients — a standing contributor to the recurring renderer memory growth, plus leaked connection tasks pinning doc copies.

findings.title: VERIFIED (B2): trigger replay + renderer recycling feedback loop can double-run provider scrapes, including the completed->waiting TOCTOU
findings.evidence: Keepalive writes recoverable 'waiting' ('Renderer was rebuilt before the sync trigger finished...', 1360-1362) on eval failure (1431-1445) or missing window (1446-1458), and the keepalive script itself emits it when the rebuilt renderer lacks __FREED_DEV_SYNC_ACTIVE_REQUEST_ID__ (1379-1382). Watcher re-dispatches via waiting_recoverable (1493-1505, 1218, 1193-1207). TOCTOU confirmed: status read at 1408-1416, eval at 1431-1432, and write_current_dev_sync_trigger_result has no terminal-status guard (1228-1244) — a 'completed' written between read and eval is overwritten with recoverable 'waiting'. The orphaned scrape command survives renderer destruction holding the scraper_session (4106-4141); a replayed dispatch queues behind it on the mutex (4126). Enabled for all dev-channel users (1267-1290: env var, debug builds, or release-channel.json channel/selected/installed == 'dev').
findings.failure_scenario: Each mid-trigger recycle replays an entire hidden-WebView scrape whose first run's results were already lost; back-to-back scrapes raise WebKit pressure, triggering more recycling — the self-reinforcing loop matching observed provider-sync instability.
findings.verification: Each mid-trigger recycle replays an entire hidden-WebView scrape whose first run's results were already lost; back-to-back scrapes raise WebKit pressure, triggering more recycling — the self-reinforcing loop matching observed provider-sync instability.

findings.title: VERIFIED (B3): relay accept loop exits permanently and silently on the first accept() error while the tray still claims sync is running
findings.evidence: start_sync_relay ends with `while let Ok((stream, addr)) = listener.accept().await` (9478-9483); any io::Error terminates the loop with no logging, retry, or restart — the function simply returns and nothing supervises the tokio task spawned at 11633-11635. Tray tooltip is the static string "Freed — Sync running" (10621).
findings.failure_scenario: Transient ECONNABORTED or fd exhaustion during heavy multi-WebView scraping kills LAN sync for the app's remaining lifetime; all PWA reconnects fail until restart while the tray reports sync healthy.
findings.verification: Transient ECONNABORTED or fd exhaustion during heavy multi-WebView scraping kills LAN sync for the app's remaining lifetime; all PWA reconnects fail until restart while the tray reports sync healthy.

findings.title: VERIFIED (B4): full-doc copy per client per broadcast plus JSON-number-array IPC for broadcast_doc, with self-echo to the pushing client
findings.evidence: Per-client send clones the whole doc despite the Arc: doc.as_ref().clone().into() at 9436 and initial push at 9401. broadcast_doc takes doc_bytes: Vec<u8> (6405-6408), which crosses Tauri IPC as a JSON number array built by Array.from(binary) in the worker (automerge.worker.ts:634-635) and invoked at automerge.ts:369. Self-echo confirmed: the connection subscribes at 9408 before storing/rebroadcasting its own push at 9414-9418, so broadcast_tx.send delivers the pushed doc back to the sender's broadcast_rx.
findings.failure_scenario: Each save costs multiples of doc size in renderer JSON strings plus N native copies per broadcast; scales unboundedly as the feed doc accretes items, concentrating allocation churn in the WebKit process on every mutation.
findings.verification: Each save costs multiples of doc size in renderer JSON strings plus N native copies per broadcast; scales unboundedly as the feed doc accretes items, concentrating allocation churn in the WebKit process on every mutation.

findings.title: VERIFIED (B5): fetch_binary_url has no size cap, no timeout, a per-call client, and a JSON-number-array return path
findings.evidence: fetch_binary_url (4454-4473) builds a fresh reqwest::Client with no .timeout() (reqwest has no default total timeout), buffers the whole body via response.bytes() with no limit, and returns Result<Vec<u8>>; the media vault consumes it as invoke<number[]> (media-vault.ts:287, fetchMediaBytes). Contrast: fetch_url enforces content-length rejection and a streaming byte cap when max_bytes is set (4233-4257).
findings.failure_scenario: Large provider videos allocate unbounded native memory and multi-hundred-MB renderer JSON strings; a stalled CDN connection hangs forever while the background job stays active, deferring scraper recycling (647-664) and blocking memory recovery.
findings.verification: Large provider videos allocate unbounded native memory and multi-hundred-MB renderer JSON strings; a stalled CDN connection hangs forever while the background job stays active, deferring scraper recycling (647-664) and blocking memory recovery.

findings.title: VERIFIED (B6): watcher re-dispatches already-terminal trigger requests after app restart because last_handled_id is in-memory only
findings.evidence: dev_sync_trigger_request_should_dispatch returns true whenever last_handled_id != Some(request_id) regardless of current_status (1209-1219); last_handled_id is a local Option<String> initialized None in the watcher task (1486). Expiry is evaluated only inside the dispatch branch (1508-1510) against the 10-min DEV_SYNC_TRIGGER_REQUEST_MAX_AGE_MS (100, 1254-1265), and the dispatch path overwrites the result file with 'started' (1548-1554) before evaling. Self-restart paths making this realistic: app.request_restart() at 10161 and 10201 with forced exit 8s later.
findings.failure_scenario: After any crash or recovery-driven self-restart within 10 minutes of a trigger, a completed/errored request is re-dispatched, overwriting its terminal result and adding duplicate hidden-WebView scrape load exactly while the app is recovering.
findings.verification: After any crash or recovery-driven self-restart within 10 minutes of a trigger, a completed/errored request is re-dispatched, overwriting its terminal result and adding duplicate hidden-WebView scrape load exactly while the app is recovering.
